attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
There's no `ui.content` module, so page content lives directly in AEM. The `/content/dell/en-us/home.html` URL refers to a **JCR content node** managed in the AEM repository — it's not stored as a file in this codebase. What this repo controls is the **templates and components** that render that page.

Here's how to trace it:

**1. The page template** — determines the page layout. Look at `ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/dell/`:

aiTitle: Locate code in Dell learning platform

content.text:
The URL `https://learning.dell.com/content/dell/en-us/home.html` maps to a **JCR content node** that lives in the AEM repository — **not in this codebase** (there's no `ui.content` module here). The content is authored in AEM directly.

What this repo **does** contain are the components and templates that *render* that page:

| What | Location |
|---|---|
| Global header (present on every page) | `ui.apps/.../template-components/globalheader/globalheader.html` |
| Global footer | `ui.apps/.../template-components/globalfooter/globalfooter.html` |
| Custom components (search, dashboard cards, etc.) | `ui.apps/.../custom-components/` |
| Java models/services powering the components | `core/src/main/java/com/dell/core/` |

To see what's actually **on** the home page (which components are placed there, what content is authored), you need to:
1. Open **CRXDE Lite** in AEM: `https://learning.dell.com/crx/de` and navigate to `/content/dell/en-us/home/jcr:content`
2. Or use **AEM Sites console** and open the home page in Edit mode

The `globalheader.html` file you have open is part of the page shell — it's included on every page including home.

message.content: that means the page is just created by component right?

content.text:
Yes, exactly. In AEM, a page like `/content/dell/en-us/home.html` is built from two layers:

**1. Template (the shell)**
- Defines the fixed structure — header, footer, page layout
- Code here: `ui.apps/.../template-components/globalheader/globalheader.html` and `globalfooter.html`

**2. Components (the content)**
- Authors drag-and-drop components onto the page in AEM's Edit mode
- Each component has its own HTML/HTL file in this repo under `ui.apps/.../components/`
- The actual *data* (which components are placed, their authored content) lives in AEM's JCR repository — not in this codebase

So the flow is:
```
URL: /content/dell/en-us/home.html
        ↓
AEM looks up the page node in JCR
        ↓
Renders the template shell (globalheader + globalfooter from this repo)
        ↓
Renders each component placed on the page (component HTL files from this repo)
        ↓
Final HTML sent to browser
```

The **component code** (HTL/HTML + Java models) lives here in the repo. The **page structure** (which components are placed where) lives in AEM. That's why there's no `ui.content` module — the authored content is managed directly in the AEM environment, not version-controlled here.
API Error: API returned an empty or malformed response (HTTP 200) — check for a proxy or gateway intercepting the request

message.content: https://learning.dell.com/content/dell/en-us/home/my-dashboard.html how to this page of the code?

content.text: Let me search for dashboard-related components in the codebase.

The same principle applies — the **page node** (`/content/dell/en-us/home/my-dashboard`) lives in AEM's JCR, but the **component code** that renders it is here in the repo.

For `my-dashboard.html`, the key component is:

**`ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/dell/components/content/dashboardcard/`**

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `dashboardcard.html` | HTL markup for the dashboard card component |
| `clientlib-card-dashboard/js/api.js` | JavaScript — API calls for dashboard data |
| `clientlib-card-dashboard/css/dash.css` | Styles for the dashboard |
| `_cq_dialog/.content.xml` | Author dialog (fields authors configure in AEM) |

To find **which components are placed** on that specific page, you'd check CRXDE Lite at:
```
/content/dell/en-us/home/my-dashboard/jcr:content
```

That will show you every component instance on the page. Then you can match the `sling:resourceType` of each node back to the component folder in this repo under `ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/dell/components/`.

content.text:
Good find. So the page content comes from an **Experience Fragment (XF)**. Here's how it works:

**Experience Fragment flow:**
```
my-dashboard.html page
    ↓ (contains an XF component)
Experience Fragment node in JCR at:
/content/experience-fragments/dell/en-us/...
    ↓ (rendered by)
ui.apps/.../emc/components/content/experiencefragment/experiencefragment.html
```

**To find the actual dashboard content code:**

1. **In AEM authoring** — click the Experience Fragment component on the page → it will show you the XF path (something like `/content/experience-fragments/dell/en-us/my-dashboard/...`)

2. **In CRXDE Lite** — navigate to that XF path to see which components are inside the fragment

3. **The XF renderer** is at:
   `ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/emc/components/content/experiencefragment/experiencefragment.html`
   — it just loads `experiencefragment.js` to resolve the fragment path, then includes the fragment's content

So the real question is: **what components are inside that Experience Fragment?** Once you open the XF in AEM (via Sites → Experience Fragments), you'll see the actual components placed there, and those component files will be in this repo.
Let me search for navigation-related components in the codebase.
The fragment path has "external" in it — the most relevant component is `aemheaderexternal`. Let me check it.

aiTitle: Locate code in Dell learning platform

content.text:
Found it. The Experience Fragment `/content/experience-fragments/dell-fragments/external/external-navigation` contains the **external header/navigation**, and the component rendering it is:

**`ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/emc/components/content/aemheaderexternal/aemheaderexternal.html`**

Key things inside that file:

| Section | What it does |
|---|---|
| Lines 1–2 | Loads `aemheader.site` and `dell.site` clientlibs (CSS/JS) |
| Lines 66–143 | The main header — Dell Technologies logo, search bar, combo box, GSS |
| Line 148–150 | Includes the top navigation via `educationservicetopnav.html` |
| Lines 202–219 | "External View Notify" modal — shown to Dell internal users accessing the external site |
| Lines 221–236 | "Change Location" modal |

The navigation items themselves (the top nav links) come from:
`ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/emc/components/content/htmltemplates/educationservicetopnav.html`

That's the file to edit if you need to change the navigation links on the external-facing pages like `my-dashboard.html`.
